problems.title: Pairwise Euclidean Distances
problems.statementMd:
Implement `pairwise_distances(x, y)`.

Given `x` of shape `(n, d)` and `y` of shape `(m, d)`, return an array `D`
of shape `(n, m)` where `D[i, j]` is the Euclidean distance between `x[i]`
and `y[j]`.

The whole point of this task is to practice broadcasting, so you must
vectorize it.
hints: Think about 2D shape of the final result and how to get it from two input vectors (1D array).

problems.title: Reverse a Vector
problems.statementMd:
Implement `reverse_vector(x)`.

Given a 1D array `x`, return its elements in reverse order (last element
first). Use slicing — no `for`/`while` loops.
hints: A slice with a negative step walks the array backwards.

problems.title: Normalize an Array
problems.statementMd:
Implement `normalize(x)`.

Return `x` z-normalized over **all** its elements: subtract the overall mean
and divide by the overall standard deviation, i.e. `(x - mean) / std`.
Vectorize it — no `for`/`while` loops. Assume `std > 0`.
hints: Compute the mean and std over the whole array, then broadcast them back.

problems.title: Subtract Each Row's Mean
problems.statementMd:
Implement `row_demean(X)`.

Given a 2D array `X` of shape `(m, n)`, return an array of the same shape
where the mean of each **row** has been subtracted from that row. Vectorize
it with broadcasting — no `for`/`while` loops.
hints: Reduce along axis=1 with keepdims=True so the per-row means broadcast back.

problems.title: Closest Value
problems.statementMd:
Implement `closest_value(x, v)`.

Given a 1D array `x` and a scalar `v`, return the element of `x` closest to
`v` (the one minimizing `|x - v|`). No `for`/`while` loops.
hints: np.abs(x - v).argmin() gives the index of the nearest element.

problems.title: Negate Values in a Range
problems.statementMd:
Implement `mask_negate(x, lo, hi)`.

Given a 1D array `x` and scalars `lo < hi`, return a **new** array equal to
`x` but with every element strictly between `lo` and `hi` negated (multiplied
by -1). Leave `x` unchanged. Use a boolean mask — no `for`/`while` loops.
hints: Build a boolean mask (x > lo) & (x < hi) and flip the sign of those entries in a copy.

problems.title: N Largest Values
problems.statementMd:
Implement `n_largest(x, n)`.

Given a 1D array `x` and an integer `n` (with `1 <= n <= len(x)`), return the
`n` largest values of `x`, **sorted in descending order**. No `for`/`while`
loops. (Hint: `np.argpartition` finds the top `n` without a full sort.)
hints: np.argpartition puts the n largest in front (unordered); take them, then sort descending.

problems.title: Replace the Max with Zero
problems.statementMd:
Implement `replace_max(x)`.

Given a 1D array `x`, return a **new** array equal to `x` but with its
maximum element replaced by `0` (leave `x` itself unchanged). If the max
occurs more than once, replace the first occurrence. No `for`/`while` loops.
hints: argmax gives the index of the maximum; copy first so the input isn't mutated.

problems.title: Rows That Are Not Constant
problems.statementMd:
Implement `rows_not_constant(X)`.

Given a 2D array `X`, return the sub-array of rows that are **not** constant
(rows whose values are not all equal), preserving their order. Use a boolean
row mask — no `for`/`while` loops.
hints: A row is constant iff its max equals its min — compare per-row max and min, then mask rows.

problems.title: Sort Rows by a Column
problems.statementMd:
Implement `sort_by_column(X, k)`.

Given a 2D array `X` and a column index `k`, return `X` with its **rows**
reordered so that column `k` is ascending. Use `argsort` + fancy indexing —
no `for`/`while` loops.
hints: argsort the chosen column to get a row order, then index the rows with it.

problems.title: Moving Average
problems.statementMd:
Implement `moving_average(a, n)`.

Given a 1D array `a` and a window length `n`, return the array of
length-`n` moving averages. The output has length `len(a) - n + 1`, where
output `i` is the mean of `a[i : i + n]`. Vectorize it — no `for`/`while`
loops (hint: prefix sums).
hints: np.cumsum gives prefix sums; a window sum is the difference of two of them.

problems.title: One-Hot Encoding
problems.statementMd:
Implement `one_hot(labels, k)`.

Given a 1D integer array `labels` with values in `[0, k)`, return a
`(len(labels), k)` array of 0/1 where row `i` has a `1.0` in column
`labels[i]` and `0.0` elsewhere. Vectorize it — no `for`/`while` loops.
hints: Index the rows with np.arange and the columns with the labels in a single assignment.
